patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 1/2] btrfs: fix false IO failure after falling back to buffered IO
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a false IO failure issue in Btrfs after falling back to buffered IO. When direct_write() encounters an EFAULT error from btrfs_dio_write(), it should retry the fault instead of immediately switching to buffered IO. The patch introduces a new logic to handle this scenario, attempting to fault in as many remaining pages as possible and retrying if progress is made. If no progress is made, it falls back to buffered IO. This change aims to prevent infinite loops that could occur when writing to a file with an mmap-ed range.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that the patch incorrectly returns -EFAULT when btrfs_direct_write() has not submitted any bytes for a dio structure, causing iomap_dio_complete() to return 0 and leading to a false IO failure. The reviewer is skeptical of this fix and suggests re-examining the original solution of doing OE split and submitting separate direct writes.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that the page fault in will not address the issue because the page cache is invalidated, and suggested re-examining the original fix of doing an OE split and submitting separate direct writes.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed skepticism about the patch's fix for false IO failures after falling back to buffered IO, suggesting that it masks the failure and is not detecting it in some cases, such as nodatasum mount option. They also noted that btrfs_dio_write() should initially fail with -EFAULT but no longer does.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed skepticism about the proposed patch to handle write buffer faults by falling back to buffered IO, suggesting that a previous approach of doing OE split and submitting separate direct writes was more effective.
review_comments.raw_body:
It doesn't return -EFAULT.

btrfs_direct_write() returned an dio pointer, although it has not 
submitted any bytes for that dio structure.

So later iomap_dio_complete() returned 0.

The full trace printk looks like this:

  dio-append-buf--2501    [008] .....   627.444860: btrfs_direct_write: 
enter, root=5 ino=257 pos=0 iov_count=4096
  dio-append-buf--2501    [008] .....   627.444905: btrfs_direct_write: 
dio complete, ret=0
  dio-append-buf--2501    [008] .....   627.444906: btrfs_direct_write: 
buffered, pos=0 iov_counter=4096
  dio-append-buf--2501    [008] .....   627.444935: btrfs_direct_write: 
exit, root=5 ino=257 pos=0 iov_count=4096 ret=-5 written=0

---

Furthermore, the page fault in won't make any difference for this 
particular case, exactly explained by the comment itself, that the page 
cache will be invalidated.

---

The buffered fallback for datacsum is masking the failure for most 
cases, and the test case itself is also hiding the failure even for 
nodatasum mount option, so we are not detecting it for a long time.

Secondly I also believed the btrfs_dio_write() should fail with -EFAULT 
initially, but that's no longer the case.

My guess is iomap dio error handling change, but will need extra digging 
to pin down the iomap commit.

---

That's also the test case I'm failing with IOMAP_DIO_BOUNCE, but I think 
we should fix the g/362 failure first, as it is already failing now.

Thanks,
Qu

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations
[PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem where the defrag_mode in the Linux kernel's page allocator fails to handle non-reclaimable allocations, such as those made with GFP_ATOMIC flags. When defrag_mode is enabled, it enforces ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT to prevent fragmentation, but this causes failures for non-reclaimable allocations that cannot invoke direct reclaim or compaction. The patch clears the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT flag and retries the allocation if it requests kswapd reclaim but cannot do direct reclaim itself. This fix addresses a large number of SLUB allocation failures under network-heavy workloads.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that defrag_mode=1 causes SLUB allocation failures for GFP_ATOMIC allocations under network-heavy workloads, agreeing that the issue is significant enough to warrant a fix.
review_comments.raw_body:
We observed it on a few of the Meta workloads that adopted
defrag_mode=1.

For the service under load there were 85509 SLUB allocation failures
messages in dmesg within 2 hours. All of them are GFP_ATOMIC allocations
for skbuff_head_cache, despite free pages being available in other
migratetype freelists (~13 GB free).

Since it is networking path from the practical point of view, this means
dropped packets, failed RPC requests, tail latency spikes and overall
service degradation.

review_comments.raw_body:

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that defrag_mode was originally developed to address higher order / THP success rates under pressure, but its implementation has structural issues, specifically that it relies on preproducing watermark buffers in contiguous pageblocks for higher orders, which can fail under competition. The reviewer expressed hope to make defrag_mode the default eventually and acknowledged some bugs and overreclaim issues still being figured out.
review_comments.raw_body:
As you may remember it was developed to help with higher order / THP
success rates under pressure.

The impetus for actually deploying it was that we saw issues with
avalanches of large page cache folios vacuuming up the higher-order
chunks; this (ironically) also led to failures on the network side.

It's kind of a structural problem. We have real preproduction buffers
for order-0 pages through the watermarks. But for higher orders we
only ensure there is at least one page. That easily fails under even
mild competition.

Since we wanted to roll defrag_mode for THP in multi-tenant systems
anyway, we figured we might as well take the plunge now and battle
test the feature this way.

defrag_mode fixes *that* issue, by preproducing watermark buffers in
contiguous pageblocks - making everything up to that order more
readily available. I'm still hoping to make it the default eventually,
which was the plan with the original huge page allocator series. As we
keep leaning into higher order requests more and more, and especially
grow the non-optional ones, we kind of need non-optional preproduction
guarantees for higher orders as well.

But there are bugs like this one, and we're still figuring out some
overreclaim issues with it in production as well. So I'm glad it's
optional for the time being ;-)

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()
[PATCH v4 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch annotates guard constructors in the Linux kernel with __nonnull(), which tells the compiler to warn when a NULL pointer is statically known to be passed. This is done by re-declaring the constructor with __nonnull() for DEFINE_GUARD() and directly annotating it for __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(). The __nonnull() macro is defined in compiler_attributes.h, following existing conventions. This change aims to improve code quality by catching potential NULL pointer issues at compile-time.

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Peter Zijlstra noted that the build robot reported an issue with clang-23 being confused by the patch, and suggested that Dmitry Ilvokhin is likely CC'd on the email.
review_comments.raw_body:
The build robot found something to hate in this one. I think you're on
Cc there. It looks to me like clang-23 is confused somehow, but who
knows.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a conflict between their __nonnull() macro and glibc's own macro, proposed three possible solutions: dropping the macro, renaming it to avoid conflict, or redefining it to match glibc's convention; they are leaning towards option 1.
review_comments.raw_body:
Seems like clang is not confused here, I was able to reproduce the problem
with GCC 11 as well.

There is a conflict with glibc's own __nonnull macro

    https://elixir.bootlin.com/glibc/glibc-2.43/source/misc/sys/cdefs.h#L560-L562

which doesn't match the one from include/linux/compiler_attributes.h.
They usually don't collide, except for User Mode Linux builds, which
include both kernel and userspace headers.

Options are:

1. Drop the __nonnull() macro from include/linux/compiler_attributes.h
   and use __attribute__((__nonnull__())) directly in
   include/linux/cleanup.h. This is a bit unfortunate, since __nonnull()
   seems like a useful shortcut, but seems like the simplest solution.

2. Rename __nonnull() to __nonnull_args() to avoid the conflict.
   A returns_nonnull attribute is supported by compilers, so the name
   fits, but it diverges from the existing naming convention in
   include/linux/compiler_attributes.h.

3. #define __nonnull(params) __attribute__((__nonnull__ params)). This
   keeps the name and is compatible with both kernel and glibc usage.
   Current call sites use __nonnull() with no arguments, which works
   identically. Future callers with specific parameter numbers would use
   __nonnull((1, 2)) with double parens, matching glibc's convention. I
   don't like this option, listed it here for the sake of completeness.

I am leaning towards option 1 so far.

review_comments.author: Miguel Ojeda
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miguel Ojeda suggested standardizing the spelling of __nonnull() across the kernel by introducing a single, consistent naming convention for this attribute, such as just 'nonnull', '__knonnull', or other variations, to avoid confusion and make maintenance easier.
review_comments.raw_body:
:(

What about other similar names? i.e. a variation of your option 2,
e.g. just `nonnull` (we also have others like that, i.e. no
underscore, e.g. `noinline`), or `___nonnull` (triple underscore, but
may be confusing), or a suffix/prefix letter, e.g. `__knonnull` (for
kernel nonnull)...

i.e. it would be nice to have a "standard" spelling for ourselves, and
also replace the existing `__attribute__((nonnull))`s we have
elsewhere in the tree.

Cheers,
Miguel

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the macro name might be a concern and is considering alternative names such as __knonnull() or __nonnull_args(), but prefers the latter due to its descriptive nature.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes, a different name might work as well. The main question is which
name to pick.

Plain nonnull() is a bit dangerous. It might collide with existing
identifiers (now or in the future) and is not great for a kernel-wide
macro. I think we got away with plain noinline, because it was there
forever. There are also at least 24 nonnull attribute usages in the
kernel that need to be converted atomically. This can be done, but it
increases the scope of the patchset, which I'd rather avoid.

___nonnull() with triple underscore might be a bit confusing, I agree.

__knonnull() might work. I like __nonnull_args() better: it gives the
reader a hint about the semantics, while __knonnull() is just a
collision-avoidance trick without conveying any additional meaning. That
being said, I can totally do __knonnull().

[PATCH v4 0/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks from unconditional guards in the Linux kernel. The checks were dead code because the guards guarantee a non-NULL lock pointer at destructor time. The patch affects several macros, including DEFINE_GUARD(), DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(), and DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_0(). It also fixes a few specific instances where the NULL check was not being used correctly. The changes are verified to have no performance impact, with a negligible reduction in code size.

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix XDR padding calculation in ff_encode_getdeviceinfo

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server where the XDR padding calculation for the da_addr_body field is incorrect, leading to stale reply-page content being leaked to the client and mis-alignment of subsequent version list decodes. The fix uses xdr_align_size() to match the actual encoded data written by xdr_encode_opaque().

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: block non-SAVEFH ops after FOREIGN PUTFH to prevent NULL deref

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: block non-SAVEFH ops after FOREIGN PUTFH to prevent NULL deref

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a potential NULL pointer dereference in the nfsd kthread when handling foreign filehandles (FHs) and inter-Server Server Copy (SSC) operations. The issue arises when a remote client crafts a COMPOUND request that includes an inter-SSC COPY with additional ops inserted between the source PUTFH and SAVEFH, causing fh_export to be NULL. To fix this, the patch introduces a gate in the dispatch loop that blocks non-SAVEFH ops after FOREIGN PUTFH if fh_dentry is NULL.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [BUG] iomap: NULL dereference in iomap_finish_folio_read after deferred failed read
Re: [PATCH] fuse: disable default bdi strictlimiting
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch disables default strictlimiting in the fuse (Filesystem in Userspace) module to prevent performance regressions caused by excessive write throttling due to large folio sizes and limited dirty budgets. The current implementation can lead to severe performance issues, including oscillating writes between freerunning and full throttling, especially on systems with limited RAM or cgroups with memory constraints. By disabling strictlimiting, the patch aims to prevent misbehaving servers from accumulating too many dirty pages, which can degrade write throughput for other filesystems.

review_comments.author: Horst Birthelmer
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Horst Birthelmer agrees with Joanne Koong's concerns about large folio sizes causing excessive throttling and performance regressions, stating that they saw similar issues when enabling large folios for testing.

Reviewer Horst Birthelmer expressed uncertainty about the best approach to handle bdi strictlimiting, suggesting that a module parameter might be a suitable solution to balance the need for limits on unprivileged servers while avoiding unnecessary restrictions.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sounds good to me, since we have seen pretty much the same when we enabled
large folios for testing.

---

Here I'm not really sure what the most logical and sane way would be.
I really don't like limits for no reason but I understand the necessity
to have limits enabled for unpriviledged servers.

Do you think a module parameter is the right way to go here?
The connection parameter might be a problem since an admin would have
to set it for a large number of mounts.

Horst

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer suggested that the folio alloc logic could modulate the folio size based on how close the system is to the dirty threshold, proposing a dynamic approach to prevent excessive throttling.

Reviewer noted that tracking partial dirty won't help free up large folios unless the dirty part is migrated to a smaller one, and questioned whether adding this complexity is worthwhile.

Reviewer noted that strictlimiting can be disabled via the /sys/class/bdi interface, and suggested introducing a config/module option to make this off-by-default behavior possible.
sentiment_signals: no clear disagreement or request for change
review_comments.raw_body:
Some handwaving: would it be possible for the folio alloc logic to
modulate the folio size based on how close are we to the dirty
threshold?

E.g. if the number of folios to reach the dirty limit is less than 10,
then start allocating smaller folios.

---

Tracking partial dirty is not going to help free up those large
folios, unless the dirty part is migrated to a smaller folio.  Not
sure if it's worth the added complexity, maybe...

---

This is already possible via the /sys/class/bdi interface.

I'm fine with introducing a config option / module option to turn it
off by default.

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that strictlimiting needs to be disabled by default for privileged servers, but expresses uncertainty about Miklos' comfort level with this approach and suggests exploring alternative solutions such as using the /sys/class/bdi interface or a module parameter.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm hoping we're able to keep things as simple as possible by just
getting rid of strictlimiting by default for privileged servers on the
kernel side, without needing any module parameters or libfuse changes,
but I'm not sure if Miklos is comfortable with that. imo if the server
is privileged, it can be trusted to not misbehave and should be given
the same kind of capabilities as a local filesystem. But I guess there
might be the possibility of user regressions if it turns out people's
privileged fuse servers are accidentally buggy.

I think the module parameter makes sense if admins trust unprivileged
servers enough to turn off strictlimiting globally. Otherwise, I think
Miklos just mentioned there's already a /sys/class/bdi interface that
lets admins disable strictlimit on a per-mount basis, so I think it
would be cleaner (though more annoying on the userspace side :)) if
libfuse used this to turn it off by default. The max supported folio
order is set per-inode when the inode is first looked up, not at mount
time, so the admin can disable strictlimit via sysfs after mount
(which is when the bdi sysfs entry gets created) and new inodes will
pick up large folio support from there.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm: list_lru: fix set_shrinker_bit() call during race with cgroup deletion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a long-standing bug in the list_lru code that causes the shrinker bit to be set on the wrong cgroup when there is a race between adding an object to the list and deleting the cgroup. The fix involves returning the effective memcg from the locking function, which allows the caller to use it instead of the original memcg. This change ensures that the shrinker bit is set correctly even in the presence of concurrent cgroup deletions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function, which is used to acquire a lock for list additions and deletions. The function will not fail to acquire the lock, so these checks are no longer needed. This change simplifies the code and makes it more efficient.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the unlock_list_lru() function by consolidating its implementation into a single inline function, eliminating duplicate code in both MEMCG and !MEMCG variants. The function is responsible for unlocking the lock associated with a list_lru_one structure, taking into account whether interrupts are disabled or not. This change simplifies the codebase and reduces maintenance overhead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for a race condition between list LRU operations and cgroup deletion to the caller of lock_list_lru_of_memcg(). This is done by removing the dead list check from the function itself, allowing for future unification of the lock_list_lru() variants. The change affects only the MEMCG variant of lock_list_lru(), which needs to handle this specific race condition.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function by moving it to a separate static inline function and removing its duplicate definition in the code. The shared function is used in both MEMCG and non-MEMCG paths, reducing code duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API. It provides normal and irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the addition and deletion functions to allow callers to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state. This is necessary because some users, such as the THP shrinker, require this synchronization to maintain consistency. The patch adds new locking functions and modifies existing ones to support this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function called folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() that allocates list_lru heads for shrinkable folios under the memory cgroup (memcg) they are associated with. The function is designed to handle the complexities of accessing the memcg of a folio, which requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. It aims to make the existence check lightweight under RCU and only pin the memcg when necessary for allocation. This patch is part of a larger series that prepares the THP shrinker to use list_lru, and it has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] mm: memory: flatten alloc_anon_folio() retry loop
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the alloc_anon_folio() function by flattening its retry loop to improve code readability and maintainability. The original top-level if statement was buried four levels deep, making it difficult to understand and modify. By moving the success path to a more visible location, the code becomes cleaner and easier to work with. This change is made in preparation for additional code that will be added in the next patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change improves handling of cgroups in a suboptimal fashion by avoiding imperfect guard rails and streamlining list operations and reclaim walks. The update also adds calls to create node heads for cgroups on demand when the first object of interest is allocated, eliminating the need for special care in migration code between nodes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series, version 5, aims to switch the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of its current implementation. The changes include fixing a long-standing issue in set_shrinker_bit(), updating the memcg lock signature, and addressing several other issues such as memory leaks and UAFs. Additionally, the patch series rebases onto the latest mm-unstable and includes various cleanups and improvements to make the conversion safe and minimally invasive.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] btrfs: skip COW for written extent buffers allocated in current transaction

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] btrfs: skip COW for written extent buffers allocated in current transaction

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch optimizes Btrfs by skipping unnecessary copy-on-write (COW) operations when a buffer is written in the current transaction. When memory pressure causes writeback of a recently COW'd buffer, Btrfs sets a flag indicating it has been written. Subsequent searches then re-COW the buffer unnecessarily, leading to COW amplification and potential performance degradation. The patch addresses this issue by re-dirtying the buffer and re-registering its range in the transaction's dirty_pages when the generation matches the current transaction. This approach ensures that the buffer is not unnecessarily re-written, while maintaining crash safety and proper handling of dirty metadata.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] btrfs: don't finalize fs roots during qgroup snapshot accounting

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Btrfs file system to prevent finalizing file system roots during qgroup snapshot accounting. The original code would prematurely clear flags and free metadata, leading to incorrect state and potential corruption of snapshots. To fix this, a new function `qgroup_commit_fs_roots()` is introduced, which iterates over dirty roots and updates their root items without triggering finalization side effects. This approach ensures that the transaction remains in progress and allows for future optimizations to rely on the FORCE_COW flag correctly.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/2] btrfs: skip COW for written extent buffers

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series optimizes Btrfs by skipping redundant copy-on-write (COW) operations on extent buffers that have already been written back to disk due to memory pressure. The optimization relies on the BTRFS_HEADER_FLAG_WRITTEN flag, which is set when a buffer is written back to disk. This prevents unnecessary re-COW of these buffers during subsequent btrfs_search_slot() calls, reducing block reservations and improving throughput under memory pressure. Additionally, the patch fixes a latent issue in qgroup snapshot accounting by separating root item persistence from finalization steps.

[PATCH v3] sched/fair: use rq_clock() in update_tg_load_avg() rate-limit

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the update_tg_load_avg() function in the Linux kernel's scheduler to use rq_clock() instead of sched_clock_cpu(). This change reduces CPU usage by eliminating the need for a read from the TSC (Time Stamp Counter) and uses a more up-to-date timestamp. The rq_clock() is considered fresh because all callers of update_tg_load_avg() hold the rq->lock and have called update_rq_clock(rq) within microseconds. This patch was tested to reduce CPU usage by about 20% in a synthetic test.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the update_tg_load_avg() function to use rq_clock_task() instead of sched_clock_cpu() for rate-limiting. This change eliminates the need for a read from the TSC (Time Stamp Counter) and aligns the rate-limit clock with the clock used for computing the load average. The result is improved performance, particularly under heavy IRQ loads where the wall time clock advances faster than the clock_task clock.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: allocate eb-attached btree pages as movable

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: allocate eb-attached btree pages as movable

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the allocation of btree pages in Btrfs to make them movable, which allows the page allocator to move them around and reduce fragmentation. The change involves passing a full GFP mask directly to functions that allocate pages, rather than having them calculate it from boolean flags. This is necessary because the current behavior causes btree_inode pages to be pinned as unmovable, even though they can be moved. The patch targets a common problem of fragmentation in Btrfs, where ~28 GB of btree_inode pages are parked across many tainted superpageblocks, preventing large allocations. With this change, those pages now land in movable pageblocks and can be drained by the background defragger.

patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH 00/40] mm: reliable 1GB page allocation

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH 00/40] mm: reliable 1GB page allocation

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 01/40] mm: page_alloc: replace pageblock_flags bitmap with struct pageblock_data
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces the packed bitmap of pageblock flags with a per-pageblock struct containing its own flags word, reducing cache line touches in the free path and simplifying accessor code. The memory overhead is 8 bytes per pageblock, but this is considered negligible. No functional changes are made.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 02/40] mm: page_alloc: per-cpu pageblock buddy allocator
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a per-cpu pageblock buddy allocator to improve scalability and reduce contention on large machines. The current zone->lock bottleneck is addressed by extending the per-CPU page cache (PCP) to operate on whole pageblocks, eliminating affinity violations and reducing lock hold times. The PCP refill process is divided into four phases: recovering owned fragments, claiming whole pageblocks, grabbing sub-pageblock chunks, and traditional __rmqueue() with migratetype fallback. This approach enables PCP-level merging, reduces fragmentation, and allows the allocator to degrade gracefully when necessary.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 03/40] mm: page_alloc: split-path PCP free with local-trylock + remote-llist
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a lock-inversion risk in the page allocator's PCP free path by introducing a per-CPU remote free list (llist) that allows remote CPUs to enqueue pages for free without taking the zone->lock. The local CPU then absorbs these queued pages back onto its PCP buddy lists at the next opportunity, eliminating the need for a trylock-fallback approach and reducing lock contention. This change also includes various other optimizations, such as memory hot-add lazy init and improved handling of page allocation on dead CPUs.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 04/40] mm: mm_init: fix zone assignment for pages in unavailable ranges
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel's memory initialization code where pages in unavailable ranges are assigned to the wrong zone. The problem occurs when an unavailable range spans multiple zones, causing later page_zone() calls to return incorrect results. To fix this, the patch looks up the correct zone for each PFN in the hole during initialization, which has no performance impact since it only runs once at boot time.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 05/40] mm: page_alloc: remove watermark boost mechanism
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the watermark boost mechanism from the Linux kernel's page allocation code. The mechanism was introduced to react to fragmentation events at the pageblock granularity, but it has been found to cause issues in workloads with high memory availability. With the introduction of superpageblocks and anti-fragmentation enforcement at 1 GiB granularity, sub-pageblock fallbacks are no longer meaningful signals. The boost is also applied before the success path is decided, leading to unnecessary wakeups of kswapd even when free pages are abundant. The patch drops the entire mechanism, removing various functions, flags, and sysctls related to watermark boosting.

Reviewer noted that removing the watermark boost mechanism would also require updating the 'boost' field in /proc/zoneinfo and show_mem output, suggesting a similar change should be made for these as well.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 06/40] mm: page_alloc: async evacuation of stolen movable pageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a lightweight asynchronous evacuation mechanism to prevent memory fragmentation caused by movable pageblocks being stolen for unmovable or reclaimable allocations. When a movable pageblock is claimed, a work item is queued to migrate the remaining movable pages out, allowing future allocations to be concentrated in fewer pageblocks and reducing fragmentation.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 07/40] mm: page_alloc: track actual page contents in pageblock flags
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends the pageblock_data flags to track the actual types of pages allocated within a pageblock, independent of its intended migratetype. The new flags PB_has_unmovable, PB_has_reclaimable, and PB_has_movable are set at allocation time and cleared on full pageblock free or when migration confirms no pages of that type remain. This allows for superpageblock-level steering decisions to direct future allocations to already-tainted regions, keeping clean regions available for large contiguous allocations.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 08/40] mm: page_alloc: superpageblock metadata for 1GB anti-fragmentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new data structure called superpageblock, which groups pageblocks at a coarser granularity of 1GB to enable anti-fragmentation tracking. The superpageblock maintains counts of pageblocks by migratetype and a list_head for future organization by fullness category. This is done to steer unmovable/reclaimable allocations into already-tainted superpageblocks, preserving clean superpageblocks for 1GB hugepage allocation. The patch adds new definitions and data structures in the Linux kernel's mmzone.h and page_alloc.c files, with negligible overhead of ~48 bytes per superpageblock.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 09/40] mm: page_alloc: support superpageblock resize for memory hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for resizing superpageblocks when memory is hotplugged. It introduces a new function `resize_zone_superpageblocks` that allocates a new superpageblock array covering the full zone span, copies existing superpageblocks, and initializes new ones for the added range. This ensures that pages in newly hotplugged regions are steered by superpageblocks, preventing them from bypassing anti-fragmentation steering entirely. The patch also adds a boolean flag `spb_kvmalloced` to track whether superpageblocks were allocated using kvmalloc (hotplug).

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 10/40] mm: page_alloc: add superpageblock fullness lists for allocation steering
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new data structure to track the fullness of superpageblocks in memory, which are groups of pageblocks that can be allocated together as a single unit. The fullness is tracked by five buckets (FULL, 75%, 50%, 25%, ALMOST_EMPTY) and two categories (CLEAN vs TAINTED), allowing for efficient allocation steering without sorting. A separate list tracks completely free superpageblocks. The patch also adds a debugfs interface to display the superpageblock information.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 11/40] mm: page_alloc: steer pageblock stealing to tainted superpageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's page allocation mechanism to prioritize stealing pages from tainted superpageblocks when allocating unmovable or reclaimable memory. The goal is to preserve clean superpageblocks for future hugepage allocations, which are typically used for large memory requests. To achieve this, the patch introduces a bounded scan of the free list to find an alternative page from a tainted superpageblock if the original candidate page belongs to a clean superpageblock. This optimization aims to reduce fragmentation and improve memory allocation efficiency.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 12/40] mm: page_alloc: steer movable allocations to fullest clean superpageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch steers movable allocations in the Linux kernel to use the fullest clean superpageblock, which helps preserve empty superpageblocks for potential huge page allocation. It introduces two new functions: `sb_preferred_for_movable` and `__rmqueue_from_sb`, which are used to find the fullest clean superpageblock and allocate pages from it. The patch also fixes an oversight in the bulk allocator's handling of ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT, ensuring that it doesn't taint clean superpageblocks. This change aims to improve memory allocation efficiency and reduce fragmentation.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 13/40] mm: page_alloc: extract claim_whole_block from try_to_claim_block
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic of claiming a whole pageblock in the Linux kernel's memory management system. It extracts this logic into a standalone function called `claim_whole_block`, which handles the transition from free to used, changes the migratetype of the pageblock, splits the block if necessary, and updates the free lists. This refactoring is done as part of a larger series of patches that aim to improve memory management in the kernel. The patch does not introduce any functional changes but prepares for future reuse of this logic in other parts of the codebase.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 14/40] mm: page_alloc: add per-superpageblock free lists
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces per-superpageblock (SPB) free lists to improve allocation steering and memory-hotplug correctness. The SPB free lists allow the allocator to target specific superpageblocks without walking the entire zone, making it more efficient. Additionally, the patch addresses two latent bugs related to memory-hotplug: one where list heads are not properly initialized on hot-add, and another where embedded free lists are not updated during resize operations. The patch takes a critical section lock to prevent concurrent access by the allocator while updating the per-SPB free lists.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 15/40] mm: page_alloc: add background superpageblock defragmentation worker
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a background worker that defragments superpageblocks when free space is low. The worker evacuates movable pages from tainted superpageblocks, improving memory utilization and reducing fragmentation. Defragmentation is triggered when a tainted superpageblock has less than two free pageblocks or three pageblocks worth of free pages. The worker uses an event-driven approach to target specific superpageblocks in need of defragmentation, rather than scanning the entire system. This patch also introduces per-superpageblock defrag work and irq work fields for memory-hotplug correctness.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 16/40] mm: compaction: walk per-superpageblock free lists for migration targets
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the compaction mechanism in Linux to walk per-superpageblock (SPB) free lists when searching for migration targets. This change is necessary because free pages are stored on per-SPB free lists, not zone-level free lists, and standard compaction's free-page scanner needs to account for this. The patch also updates the pageblock movability bookkeeping to ensure it stays correct during compaction. Additionally, it fixes a check for zones whose start_pfn is not aligned to pageblock_order.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 17/40] mm: page_alloc: superpageblock-aware contiguous and higher order allocation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new allocation strategy that leverages superpage block (SPB) metadata to find contiguous ranges of free pages, making large and contiguous allocations more efficient. It adds tracking of contiguous allocations in SPB metadata and modifies the compaction algorithm to use this information for THP/mTHP and high-order unmovable/reclaimable allocations. The patch also includes a fallback mechanism to traditional compaction if the SPB approach fails.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 18/40] mm: page_alloc: prevent atomic allocations from tainting clean SPBs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents atomic allocations from tainting clean superpageblocks (SPBs) by introducing a staged relaxation of the allocation process. The patch extends the __rmqueue_smallest function to search for sub-pageblock-order free blocks in tainted SPBs, and introduces an intermediate flag ALLOC_NOFRAG_TAINTED_OK that allows retrying with tainted SPBs before dropping protection entirely. Additionally, it bypasses the pageblock compatibility threshold when trying to claim a pageblock from a tainted SPB. The patch also preserves the behavior for callers that pass __GFP_NORETRY, which is intended to handle failures by returning NULL and letting the caller's fallback path take over.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 19/40] mm: page_alloc: aggressively pack non-movable allocs in tainted SPBs on large systems
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new threshold, SPB_AGGRESSIVE_THRESHOLD, to control when the Linux kernel aggressively packs non-movable allocations into tainted superpageblocks (SPBs) on large systems. On systems with more than 8 superpageblocks, it relaxes the minimum order floor for tainted SPBs, allowing non-movable allocations to consume free space at any granularity. This is done to prevent unnecessary tainting of clean SPBs and preserve MOVABLE capacity within tainted SPBs. The patch preserves the original behavior on small systems with fewer superpageblocks.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 20/40] mm: page_alloc: prefer reclaim over tainting clean superpageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory allocation algorithm to prefer reclaiming pages over tainting clean superpageblocks when allocating memory. The change aims to prevent unnecessary tainting of superpageblocks, which can lead to fragmentation and decreased performance. To achieve this, the patch reorders the allocation fallback cascade to try reclaim and compaction before tainting clean superpageblocks. It also preserves the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT flag through the slowpath and drops it as a last resort when OOM is reached. Testing shows that this change maintains unmovable pageblocks at zero throughout heavy workloads, where previously 2-3 additional SPBs would become tainted.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 21/40] mm: page_alloc: adopt partial pageblocks from tainted superpageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the page allocation mechanism in Linux to adopt partially free pageblocks from tainted superpageblocks during refilling of the per-cpu page pool (PCP). It introduces a new phase in the multi-phase approach used by rmqueue_bulk, which searches for partially-free pageblocks with sub-pageblock buddy entries matching the requested migratetype. The patch aims to reduce fragmentation spread to clean superpageblocks and concentrates non-movable allocations into already-tainted superpageblocks.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 22/40] mm: page_alloc: add CONFIG_DEBUG_VM sanity checks for SPB counters
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds sanity checks to the Linux kernel's page allocation code to ensure that per-superpageblock counters are accurate. It introduces a new function `spb_debug_check` that verifies that the counters for free, unmovable, reclaimable, and movable pages do not exceed the total number of pageblocks. The function is called after each operation that modifies these counters. Additionally, three existing warnings in the kernel's code have been relaxed to prevent spurious warnings when migrating or isolating pages.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 23/40] mm: page_alloc: targeted evacuation and dynamic reserves for tainted SPBs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch reduces the proliferation of tainted superpageblocks by introducing dynamic reserves and targeted evacuation strategies. The reserve size is scaled based on the superpageblock size, allowing for more efficient use of memory. Before dropping ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT, the patch attempts to evacuate movable pages from tainted superpageblocks to prevent tainting clean ones. This approach aims to reduce memory fragmentation and improve performance.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 24/40] mm: page_alloc: prevent UNMOVABLE/RECLAIMABLE mixing in pageblocks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the mixing of UNMOVABLE and RECLAIMABLE pages in pageblocks by tightening two paths in the page allocator: try_to_claim_block() and __rmqueue_steal(). The patch introduces a helper function noncompatible_cross_type() to detect the UNMOVABLE-RECLAIMABLE pair, and modifies these functions to prevent cross-type relabeling or stealing. This change aims to reduce internal fragmentation caused by sticky RECLAIMABLE pages in pageblocks with both types of pages.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 25/40] mm: trigger deferred SPB evac when atomic allocs would taint a clean SPB
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a mechanism to trigger deferred superpageblock (SPB) evacuation when an atomic allocation would otherwise taint a clean SPB. The goal is to prevent the tainting of clean SPBs by allowing the next allocation to claim from the tainted pool instead. This is achieved by scheduling a deferred spb_evacuate_for_order() on the zone's pgdat workqueue whenever a non-movable allocation attempts to claim a pageblock from an empty or clean superpageblock as a fallback. The trigger is gated by single-flight, throttle, and tainted-pool precheck inside queue_spb_evacuate(), making it safe to fire from this hot path without storming the workqueue.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 26/40] mm: page_alloc: refuse fragmenting fallback for callers with cheap fallback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's page allocation mechanism to refuse fragmentation fallbacks for certain callers, specifically those that have a cheap fallback path. The change is made to prevent early-boot failures in QEMU due to a coarse bail-out gate in the slowpath retry of get_page_from_freelist. A finer-grained refusal is introduced in __rmqueue, where it checks if the allocation can be absorbed by a tainted pageblock or free pages. If so, it returns NULL and increments the SPB_HIGHORDER_REFUSED counter. The patch also adds a new flag ALLOC_HIGHORDER_OPTIONAL to indicate callers with cheap fallback paths.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 27/40] mm: page_alloc: cross-migratetype buddy borrow within tainted SPBs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces two new passes in the page allocation process to allow cross-migratetype buddy borrowing within tainted Special Purpose Buffers (SPBs). The first pass, Pass 2c, allows UNMOV and RECL allocations to borrow from each other's free lists. The second pass, Pass 2d, enables non-movable allocations to borrow from the MOVABLE free list of a tainted SPB. This is done to reduce structural mismatches that can lead to fragmentation and wasted space in the system. The patch restricts these new passes to tainted SPBs only, ensuring that clean SPBs are not polluted with cross-type mixing.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 28/40] mm: page_alloc: drive slab shrink from SPB anti-fragmentation pressure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new slab shrink mechanism to address anti-fragmentation pressure in the Linux kernel. The mechanism is triggered by various events, including demand and supply pressures, as well as proactive high-water marks. It uses a per-pgdat irq_work to bridge from allocator-lock context to a workqueue, which calls shrink_slab() with the zone's nid to free objects until either no progress is made or a batch of pageblock-sized objects has been freed. This approach aims to prevent sustained slab growth and fragmentation by releasing pressure on tainted SPBs, allowing for more efficient memory management.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 29/40] mm: page_reporting: walk per-superpageblock free lists
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the page reporting cycle to correctly walk per-superpageblock free lists, which were introduced by the superpage rework. The previous implementation walked an empty zone-level list, causing virtio-balloon free page reporting to become a no-op on systems with superpageblocks. The patch introduces a new function `page_reporting_cycle_list` that takes an explicit list_head and a shared budget pointer, allowing it to iterate over all superpageblocks in the zone for the requested order and memory type. The patch also holds the memory hotplug read lock around the SPB walk to prevent dangling references. A fallback path is retained for zones whose SPB array has not yet been allocated.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 30/40] mm: show_mem: collect migratetype letters from per-superpageblock lists
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the show_mem function to correctly display migratetype letters for each order in a zone. After the superpage block (SPB) rework, free pages are stored on per-superpageblock lists instead of zone-level free_area lists. The patch iterates over each SPB in the zone and checks if any migratetypes have free pages at that order. If all migratetypes have been seen, it exits early. The patch also handles zones whose SPB array has not yet been allocated by falling back to the zone-level free_area. This change ensures that the migratetype letters are accurately displayed in the show_mem output.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 31/40] mm: page_alloc: per-(zone, order, mt) PASS_1 hint cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a hint cache to improve the performance of page allocations in the Linux kernel. The cache stores the most recently used superpage block (SPB) for each zone, order, and migratetype tuple, allowing the kernel to quickly retrieve the next SPB to allocate from. This reduces the number of iterations required during page allocation, resulting in a significant reduction in walk depth and an increase in hint hit rate. The patch also includes measurements showing a 99% hint hit rate and a median walk depth reduction from ~50 SPBs to ~5 SPBs on a test system with a steady workload.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 32/40] mm: debug: prevent infinite recursion in dump_page() with CMA
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents infinite recursion in the dump_page() function when dealing with Contiguous Memory Allocation (CMA). It does this by adding a guard to check if the page frame number is valid before resolving its zone and classifying it. This ensures that the function can safely report on pages without a meaningful zone, preventing the kernel from running out of stack due to recursive calls.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 33/40] PM: hibernate: walk per-superpageblock free lists in mark_free_pages
series_items.patch_summary: This RFC patch proposes a change to the hibernate function mark_free_pages() to handle the new superpageblock (SPB) free lists introduced by the SPB rework. The existing per-page walk is wrapped in an SPB iteration loop, and when there are no SPBs (e.g., in unpopulated hotplug zones), it falls back to the zone-level free list.

review_comments.author: Rafael J. Wysocki
review_comments.summary: Suggested breaking down the code into a separate function to improve readability, and proposed a specific refactoring approach.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 34/40] btrfs: allocate eb-attached btree pages as movable
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Btrfs file system to allocate extent buffer pages attached to btree_inode as movable, allowing them to be migrated and reducing fragmentation in the page-superblock allocator. The change targets a dominant source of fragmentation observed on large test systems, where ~28 GB of btree_inode pages were parked across many tainted superpageblocks, preventing 1 GiB hugepage allocation from those regions. By adding the __GFP_MOVABLE flag to one allocation site and passing it through to other functions, the patch enables these pages to be moved to movable pageblocks, where they can be drained by the background defragger without needing a fallback LRU-sample approach.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Boris Burkov pointed out that the patch's assumption about extent buffers being eligible for release_folio() after refs == 1 is incorrect, as they should be immediately released when a block gets cowed, and instead suggested that the folios of interest are those with refs == 1 that will return 0 to release_folio(), which can already be reclaimed by memory pressure based reclaim.

Reviewer Boris Burkov suggested adding a Reviewed-by tag and left a small code nit, but did not raise any technical concerns about the patch's functionality.

Reviewer Boris Burkov suggested that instead of passing a __GFP_MOVABLE boolean flag, the code should transparently pass along a GFP flag to let callers handle movable pages correctly.
review_comments.raw_body:
Small correction to a generally correct story:

every extent_buffer we have used (it is newly cowed or we have read it
for some reason) will settle into a state where extent_buffer->refs == 1
and it is eligible for release_folio(). If a block gets cowed, then its
extent_buffer should be fully released/freed right away, with a tricky
EXTENT_BUFFER_STALE bit dance to prevent accidental reuse during this
process.

So I don't think cow or trees moving around is all that relevant here.

The folios you are after are the ones which are sitting around with
extent_buffer->refs == 1 and which will return 0 to release_folio(),
which is any valid, non-stale extent_buffer no btrfs operation is
currently using (typically referred to from a btrfs_path). These are
already available to be reclaimed by memory pressure based reclaim,
drop_caches, etc.

---

I left a small code nit inline, but whether you take that suggestion or
leave it, you can add:

Reviewed-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Thanks for the patch,
Boris

---

My gut says it is nicer to just transparently pass along a gfp flag
rather than a carefully documented movable boolean and let the callers
do the right thing. Just a nit, I don't care strongly about it.

review_comments.author: David Sterba
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David Sterba agreed that GFP flags would be improved and more self-documenting in the functions where they are called, but noted that this patch is not standalone and suggested cleaning it up later to allow the series to proceed.
review_comments.raw_body:
Agreed, the GFP flags would be better and also a bit more self
documenting where the functions are called. The patch is not standalone
so I don't mind having the booleans, we can clean it up later to let the
40-patch series proceed.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: Author agrees that the patch could be submitted independently and is willing to rework it by passing GFP masks from each caller, planning to send a revised version soon.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think this patch could go in by itself. It doesn't
depend on anything else in the series.

I sent it with this series mostly because it's needed
to make the series work, and to provide context on why
it's needed.

I'm happy to resend it with a GFP mask passed in by
each caller. That would look better, indeed!

I'll send you guys a cleaned up patch this coming week.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Matthew Wilcox expressed concern that the patch's use of GFP_NOFS is a code smell, suggesting it should be replaced with memalloc_nofs_save/restore calls instead. He recommended lifting the GFP_NOFS to the callers and passing in GFP_NOFS | __GFP_MOVABLE (or plain GFP_NOFS) for now, allowing for future changes to GFP_KERNEL where warranted.
review_comments.raw_body:
If you're respinning anyway ...

Every use of GFP_NOFS is a bad code smell.  Filesystems should be using
memalloc_nofs_save/restore.  The problem is that it takes deep filesystem
knowledge to understand where the memalloc_nofs_save() should start.
It would be good to lift the GFP_NOFS to the callers of this function as
it gets us slightly closer to being able to replace it with GFP_KERNEL
in the places it's not actually needed.

ie what I'm asking for is, please don't do the 'extra_gfp' thing.
Just pass in gfp from the callers and make them all pass in GFP_NOFS |
__GFP_MOVABLE (or a plain GFP_NOFS) for now.  Someone can come along
later and change them to GFP_KERNEL later where warranted.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested that the patch could be improved by bubbling up the __GFP_MOVABLE flag to a higher layer, which would make it more useful and reduce the need for manual intervention.
review_comments.raw_body:
Note that is should be pretty trivial to just bubble it up a layer
for changes like this.  This will naturally gravitate to more useful
places.  To make it fully correct it will need manual intervention,
but generally bubbling up should improve the situation.

Same for GFP_NOIO.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 35/40] mm: page_alloc: refuse best-effort high-order allocs servable at lower orders
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents best-effort high-order memory allocations from succeeding when a lower order allocation would be sufficient. It detects this pattern by checking if the allocation request has __GFP_NOWARN set and __GFP_NOFAIL not set, and then checks if the tainted pool can plausibly serve a smaller or same order allocation on the caller's retry. If so, it refuses the current attempt instead of escalating. This fix is intended to prevent high-order allocations from succeeding when they have an order-decrement fallback, which would otherwise lead to unnecessary escalation.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 36/40] mm: page_alloc: set ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT on alloc_frozen_pages_nolock_noprof
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT flag to the alloc_frozen_pages_nolock_noprof function in the Linux kernel's page allocator. This flag steers the allocation towards tainted-SPB sub-pageblock space before fragmenting clean superpageblocks, which helps prevent atomic kmalloc_nolock callers from tainting hugepages unnecessarily. The patch addresses a performance issue where these callers would hit the fallback path on every PCP miss and convert movable pageblocks to UNMOVABLE. The flag is purely a steering hint and does not introduce any sleeping locks or depend on __GFP_DIRECT_RECLAIM, making it safe for use in various contexts such as NMI, hardirq, and PREEMPT_RT.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 37/40] mm: page_alloc: move spb_get_category and spb_tainted_reserve to mmzone.h
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves two helper functions, `spb_get_category` and `spb_tainted_reserve`, from `mm/page_alloc.c` to `include/linux/mmzone.h`. The move is done to keep a single source of truth for these functions, which will be needed in multiple places including `mm/compaction.c`. The functions remain unchanged semantically. They are used to classify superpageblocks as clean or tainted based on their contents and to calculate the minimum reserve required by a tainted superpageblock to maintain allocatability without tainting fresh clean superpageblocks.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 38/40] mm: compaction: skip empty tainted superpageblocks as migration source
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the Linux kernel's page migration process by skipping empty tainted superpageblocks as a potential source of migratable pages. The patch introduces a fast-path check in the isolate_migratepages_block function to quickly return if a tainted superpageblock has no movable content, thus avoiding unnecessary per-page scanning work. This optimization preserves correctness for tainted superpageblocks with movable content and reduces overhead for those without.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 39/40] mm: compaction: respect tainted SPB reserve in destination selection
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's compaction algorithm to respect tainted superpageblocks (SPBs) when selecting destination pages for migration. The goal is to prevent compaction from eroding the reserve headroom in tainted SPBs, which is necessary for satisfying non-movable allocations without tainting fresh clean SPBs. The change introduces a check that refuses tainted-SPB pageblocks as compaction destinations once the number of free pages falls below the reserved amount. This bias towards producing free pageblocks inside clean SPBs can help with hugepage allocation and, combined with other changes, enables kcompactd to handle clean-SPB consolidation alone.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 40/40] mm: page_alloc: SPB tracepoint instrumentation [DO-NOT-MERGE]
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds instrumentation to the Linux kernel's page allocation mechanism using tracepoints. It introduces new tracepoints to track various events related to memory allocation, including anti-fragmentation diagnostics. The patch bundles all these tracepoint definitions and call sites into a single commit for easier review before upstream submission. The instrumentation is designed for diagnostic purposes only, providing detailed information about memory allocation decisions and behavior.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the allocation of large memory blocks by dividing memory into superpageblocks and steering unmovable, reclaimable, and high-atomic allocations into those already tainted by such allocations. The goal is to leave as many 1GB superpageblocks available for movable allocations, which can be easily defragmented for regular or huge pages. Various strategies are employed to achieve this, including preferential allocation from tainted blocks, fallback to small pages for certain kernel allocations, and migration of movable memory when a block falls below a free threshold. This approach enables more extensive use of 2MB THPs and mTHPs, as well as reliable allocation of 1GB pages. The series is built on top of Johannes's PCPBuddy series, which aims to reduce the need for CPUs to get pages from zone free lists by relying on page block ownership.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that the zone ID passed to init_unavailable_range() may not match the actual zone spanning each PFN in memory holes between memblock regions, which can cause incorrect page flags and crashes. The author fixed this by looking up the correct zone for each PFN in the hole, which has no performance impact since it's init-only code running once at boot.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about hotplugged memory not being covered by superpageblocks, acknowledged the issue, and explained how they plan to fix it in a new function called resize_zone_superpageblocks(). This function will be called from move_pfn_range_to_zone() after the zone span has been updated. It will allocate a new superpageblock array with kvmalloc_node(), copy existing superpageblocks, and initialize new superpageblocks for the added range.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about memory fragmentation caused by stealing movable pageblocks for unmovable or reclaimable allocations, and responded by adding an asynchronous evacuation mechanism to migrate remaining movable pages out of the claimed block, allowing future unmovable/reclaimable allocations to be concentrated in fewer pageblocks.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about accurately tracking page contents in superpageblocks by extending the pageblock_data flags to include PB_has_unmovable, PB_has_reclaimable, and PB_has_movable bits. The new flags are set at allocation time and cleared on full pageblock free or when migration confirms no pages of that type remain. This change provides a foundation for superpageblock-level steering decisions.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about lock inversion in the PCP free path, acknowledged that using spin_trylock and falling back to free_one_page() defeats the per-CPU pageset benefits, and proposed a solution involving a per-CPU remote free list (llist) consumed by the owning CPU. The author agreed to implement this change.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the watermark_boost mechanism, which was causing excessive wakeups of kswapd due to sub-pageblock fallbacks and order-0 reclaim. The author agreed that this mechanism is not effective for superpageblocks and decided to drop it entirely, removing various related code paths.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the overhead of tracking superpageblocks, explaining that the array is allocated per-zone during boot via memblock and has negligible overhead (~48 bytes per superpageblock). The author confirmed that this is pure bookkeeping with no allocation behavior change.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about memory overhead and storage efficiency in the pageblock flags bitmap, agreed to replace it with a per-pageblock struct containing its own flags word, which eliminates bit-packing indexing and simplifies accessor code; no functional change is expected.

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about locking contention on large machines, explained that the proposed solution extends the page cluster (PCP) to operate on whole pageblocks with ownership tracking, and confirmed that this change will eliminate affinity violations and shorten zone->lock hold time. The author also noted that the allocator degrades gracefully to traditional behavior when PCP batch sizes are small or the zone is fragmented.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Boris Burkov pointed out that the patch's focus on cow and tree movement is misplaced, as extent_buffers with refs == 1 are already eligible for release_folio() and can be reclaimed by memory pressure based reclaim or drop_caches. He suggested that the actual issue is with valid, non-stale extent_buffers not currently in use by btrfs operations, which should be available for reclaim.

Reviewer Boris Burkov suggested a minor code improvement and provided a Reviewed-by tag, indicating that he has reviewed the patch.

Reviewer Boris Burkov suggested simplifying the patch by using GFP flags instead of a movable boolean, stating that this would allow callers to handle allocations correctly without additional documentation.
review_comments.raw_body:
Small correction to a generally correct story:

every extent_buffer we have used (it is newly cowed or we have read it
for some reason) will settle into a state where extent_buffer->refs == 1
and it is eligible for release_folio(). If a block gets cowed, then its
extent_buffer should be fully released/freed right away, with a tricky
EXTENT_BUFFER_STALE bit dance to prevent accidental reuse during this
process.

So I don't think cow or trees moving around is all that relevant here.

The folios you are after are the ones which are sitting around with
extent_buffer->refs == 1 and which will return 0 to release_folio(),
which is any valid, non-stale extent_buffer no btrfs operation is
currently using (typically referred to from a btrfs_path). These are
already available to be reclaimed by memory pressure based reclaim,
drop_caches, etc.

---

I left a small code nit inline, but whether you take that suggestion or
leave it, you can add:

Reviewed-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Thanks for the patch,
Boris

---

My gut says it is nicer to just transparently pass along a gfp flag
rather than a carefully documented movable boolean and let the callers
do the right thing. Just a nit, I don't care strongly about it.

review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Usama Arif questioned the performance implications of kernel allocations falling back to small pages, specifically asking if this would result in a performance impact and whether it would also affect 2M THP allocation efficiency due to increased fragmentation of kernel memory.

Reviewer questioned whether the patch's strategy for handling movable allocation failures would result in an Out-Of-Memory (OOM) condition.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Rik!

The comments are just based on coverletter.

Hopefully will get to review all the patches. The above one of
kernel allocations falling back to small pages is interesting.

- Will it result in a performance impact as kernel allocations
wont benefit from higher order allocation?
- Will this impact 2M THP allocation efficiency due to more
fragmentation of kernel memory?

by allowed, do you mean if movable allocations fail, it will

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that the current solution may not be sufficient and suggests exploring an alternative approach to limit the number of 1GB blocks being spilled over into simultaneously, particularly when partially used for kernel memory.

Author acknowledges that the increased availability of clean 1GB page blocks will make THP (Transparent Huge Pages) allocation easier due to more movable memory being available.

The author is addressing a concern about the potential for reserved-for-unmovable memory areas to exceed the zone low watermark, leading to early OOMs. The author acknowledges this as a bad situation and suggests that protection against it would be needed if it were to happen.
review_comments.raw_body:
It might! We may well need a better solution
here, like spilling over earlier, but limiting
the number of 1GB blocks we can spill over into
simultaneously (partially used for kernel memory).

---

THPs come from movable memory. With more 1GB
page blocks not having kernel allocations in
it, THP allocations should be easier.

---

Yes, but by that time the zone free memory should
also be below the low watermark, because there 
should only be a few partially occupied tainted
1GB page blocks.

If the zone has 300MB low watermark, and there
are 50MB tied up in those reserved-for-unmovable
memory areas, it should not cause early OOMs.

I don't know if we can end up in a situation
where somehow the reserved-for-unmovable memory
would add up to more than the zone low watermark.

That would be bad, and if it happened we would
have to add some sort of protection against that.


-- 
All Rights Reversed.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the use of GFP_NOFS in the patch is a bad code smell, as it requires deep filesystem knowledge to understand where memalloc_nofs_save() should start. The reviewer suggested lifting the GFP_NOFS usage to the callers of this function and passing in GFP_NOFS | __GFP_MOVABLE (or plain GFP_NOFS) instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
If you're respinning anyway ...

Every use of GFP_NOFS is a bad code smell.  Filesystems should be using
memalloc_nofs_save/restore.  The problem is that it takes deep filesystem
knowledge to understand where the memalloc_nofs_save() should start.
It would be good to lift the GFP_NOFS to the callers of this function as
it gets us slightly closer to being able to replace it with GFP_KERNEL
in the places it's not actually needed.

ie what I'm asking for is, please don't do the 'extra_gfp' thing.
Just pass in gfp from the callers and make them all pass in GFP_NOFS |
__GFP_MOVABLE (or a plain GFP_NOFS) for now.  Someone can come along
later and change them to GFP_KERNEL later where warranted.

review_comments.author: Li,Rongqing(ACG CCN)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Li,Rongqing pointed out that while the boot-time ordering issue is a generic problem, many architectures have already fixed it internally, and using early_param() to manage static keys is still accepted in the current kernel core. They argued that using __setup() would miss tracking for critical early-stage memory pools, making early_param() the most robust option.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for your review!

While this boot-time ordering used to be a generic issue, it seems many
architectures have already aligned or fixed this internally. For instance,

commit ca829e05d3d4 ("powerpc/64: Init jump labels before parse_early_param()")
and commit 6070970db9fe ("m68k: Initialize jump labels early during setup_arch()")
explicitly relocated jump_label_init() before the early parameter parsing.

Furthermore, leveraging early_param() to directly manage static keys is still
actively used and accepted in the current core kernel. Some examples include:

  - early_param("randomize_kstack_offset", early_randomize_kstack_offset);
  - early_param("threadirqs", setup_forced_irqthreads);

The primary reason for using early_param() here instead of __setup() is that
mempool allocations can happen extremely early during the boot phase. Moving
this to a later stage like __setup() would mean missing the tracking for the
most critical early-stage memory pools, which defeats the purpose of boot-time
debugging.

Therefore, I think using early_param() here is the most robust option to
ensure full coverage of mempool allocations.

What do you think?

Thanks

-Li
